summary: Gruber responds to Jason Snell's proposal that Apple should allow sideloading on iPhone OS to counter criticism that the platform is too closed. While Gruber says he'd personally welcome sideloading, he argues it wouldn't silence critics because iPhone detractors have 'seldom let facts get in their way.' He points out that the real problem is perception, not policy — people already wrongly believe the iPad can't read PDFs or play non-iTunes media. Instead of adding sideloading, Gruber argues Apple should better promote the openness that already exists: MP3 playback, H.264 video support, and especially HTML5 web apps as a fully open alternative to the App Store.
key_points: Sideloading wouldn't silence critics because iPhone opponents routinely ignore existing facts about the platform's openness
The perception problem cuts both ways: if people think iPads can't read PDFs, they'd also miss a buried sideloading option
Apple's real problem is marketing its existing openness, not adding new open features
HTML5 web apps represent a completely open platform right alongside the App Store that Apple undersells
A web app store should come from the community, not Apple, but Apple should maintain a directory and promote WebKit's capabilities
money_quotes: iPhone critics have seldom let facts get in their way.
The question of whether the App Store is too closed is not what Snell is talking about. He's talking about the iPhone OS being perceived as 'too closed' even in areas where it is in fact wide open.
The App Store is great and popular, but the iPhone OS is more than the App Store.
A better strategy would be for Apple to do a better job promoting the ways the iPhone already is open.

Apple needs to make more people aware that right across the street from the App Store's beautiful but tightly controlled and regulated walled garden, there's a very nice public park, open to everyone.
key_insight: Apple's openness problem is one of perception and marketing, not policy — the platform is already more open than critics and even potential customers realize.
